raw_case:
Appearances Can Be Deceiving: A Case Report of Asymptomatic Appendiceal Goblet Cell Adenocarcinoma Presenting as an Ovarian Tumor


Abstract



Introduction

Goblet cell adenocarcinoma (GCA) of the appendix is an uncommon type of cancer that includes both epithelial and neuroendocrine features, with goblet cells present. These tumors have traditionally been viewed as one of the more aggressive forms of appendiceal cancer, frequently being diagnosed at a metastatic stage. For patients with stage III–IV disease, the 5-year overall survival rate ranges from 14% to 22%. Due to limited data, the diagnosis and management of GCA are challenging.

Case Presentation

We present the case of a 55-year-old female who presented with elevated serum carcinoembryonic antigen levels and a left ovarian tumor. Preoperative imaging indicated a normal appendix. During surgery for suspected ovarian carcinoma, frozen section analysis revealed mucinous adenocarcinoma of the ovary, but distinguishing between primary and metastatic lesions was challenging. Meticulous exploration revealed a firm and thickened appendix, prompting appendectomy and subsequent diagnosis of appendiceal GCA with ovarian metastasis.

Conclusion

Pathologies of the appendix, like GCA, can mimic ovarian tumors despite a normal-looking appendix, complicating diagnosis. Through this case, we underscored the need for careful intraoperative examination of the appendix in patients with mucinous ovarian neoplasms to avoid misdiagnosis and ensure appropriate treatment.

Introduction

Goblet cell adenocarcinoma (GCA) is a rare type of primary appendiceal adenocarcinoma, with a yearly incidence of 0.01–0.05 cases per 100,000 people. It exhibits characteristics of both mucinous and neuroendocrine differentiation [1]. Depending on its grade and the extent of invasion, GCA can present in various clinical ways, such as acute appendicitis, abdominal pain, or the presence of an abdominal mass [2]. Consequently, many patients may initially present to gynecologic oncologists with a presumptive diagnosis of ovarian cancer (OC).Given its insidious clinical presentation, distinguishing primary OC from metastatic carcinoma originating from appendiceal GCA poses a significant challenge, especially when the initial organ appears normal. However, within the field of gynecology, the utility of performing an appendectomy in early-stage mucinous ovarian cancer (MOC) remains a topic of contention. Some gynecologic oncologists recommend including routine appendectomy in surgeries for MOC, as it can help differentiate between a primary MOC and metastatic mucinous carcinoma by excising the appendix [3]. The CARE Checklist has been completed by the authors for this case report, attached as online supplementary material (for all online suppl. material, see https://doi.org/10.1159/000541626).

Case Presentation

A 55-year-old female (para 2) presented to our hospital due to abnormal findings in her recent health check, which revealed increased serum carcinoembryonic antigen levels and the presence of a left ovarian tumor. Laboratory tests showed that carcinoembryonic antigen was elevated at 34 ng/mL and alpha-fetoprotein level was mildly raised at 10.2 ng/mL.The other serum tests, including carbohydrate antigen 125 and 19-9, were within normal limits. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a solid tumor in the left ovary, measuring 62 × 44 mm. Positron emission tomography/computed tomography showed a maximum standardized uptake value of 4.18 in the left ovarian tumor, posing challenges in determining malignancy. Coronal computed tomography image demonstrating the appendix is of normal caliber (Fig. 1). Preoperative CT scans indicated that the appendix appears normal. Gastroscopy and colonoscopy were performed, with no suspicious lesions identified.Abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy were performed due to suspected OC. Intraoperatively, a 9-cm multinodular gray-white tumor was identified in the left ovary, with no other abnormal appearances noted in the pelvic region. Frozen section returned as mucinous adenocarcinoma of the ovary, but distinguishing primary from metastatic lesions proved difficult. Additionally, partial omentectomy, pelvic, and para-aortic lymphadenectomy were performed. During retroperitoneal lymph node dissection, we observed that the appendix felt firm and thickened compared to its usual appearance, despite appearing normal. An appendectomy was performed, and a frozen section analysis identified goblet cell carcinoma of the appendix with metastasis to the ovary. Ileocecal resection with D3 lymph node dissection was performed by a digestive surgeon. Histologically, goblet-like cells exhibit proliferation in nests and solid patterns. Immunohistochemically, the tumor cells are positive for CK7, CK20, and CDX2, and negative for chromogranin A and synaptophysin (Fig. 2). The final pathological diagnosis for the patient indicated appendiceal GCA with D3 lymph node involvement and metastases to the ovaries, staging it as pT4aN1bM1a (AJCC/UICC TNM 8th). She underwent seven cycles of XELOX (capecitabine and oxaliplatin) chemotherapy without any signs of recurrence.

Discussion

GCA is an amphicrine tumor of the appendix, mainly made up of goblet-like mucinous cells along with a small number of endocrine and Paneth-like cells, first identified by Gagne et al. [4] in 1969. In 2019, the World Health Organization updated the preferred terminology to GCA, reflecting the increasing recognition of a frequently coexisting high-grade adenocarcinoma component. Before this update, different terms like goblet cell carcinoid and adenocarcinoma ex-goblet cell carcinoid were used to describe this neoplasm [5].Diagnosing GCA before surgery is challenging because it diffusely infiltrates the appendix wall and may not form an identifiable mass detectable through gross examination or radiographic imaging [6]. Standard imaging methods such as CT scans, magnetic resonance images, or ultrasounds may not clearly detect GCA due to its diffuse nature. Functional imaging techniques are less effective in diagnosing GCA because its tendency not to express high levels of somatostatin receptors. Positron emission tomography/computed tomography shows promise because of its different mechanisms of detecting metabolic activity, although the evidence supporting this is limited [7].GCA is typically diagnosed incidentally through microscopic examination of the appendix, making an appendectomy necessary. However, gynecologists may hesitate to perform an appendectomy when GCA presents as early-stage MOC with a normal-appearing appendix, due to the ongoing controversy surrounding the necessity of appendectomy during surgery for MOC.Ramirez et al. [8] evaluated 57 patients (stage I–II) who underwent appendectomy at the time of primary cytoreduction and found that no patient had evidence of macroscopic or microscopic metastases in the appendix. The authors indicated that routine appendectomy at the time of surgery for apparent early-stage MOC is not warranted. In contrast, Lavecchia et al. [9] reported a different finding. Appendectomy during surgery for mucinous ovarian tumors revealed 6.0% having a neuroendocrine tumor or low-grade appendiceal neoplasm in retrospective study on 225 patients with MOC. The authors advocated for appendectomy with mesoappendix removal in all MOC cases, regardless of intraoperative findings, due to the limited predictive value of normal appendix appearance for appendiceal pathology [9].For patients with ovarian metastasis, immunohistochemical stains can assist in differentiating between primary MOC and metastatic ovarian tumors originating from undiagnosed appendiceal neoplasms. MOC generally exhibits a CK7+/CK20−/MUC2−/CDX2− profile, while mucinous colorectal and appendiceal adenocarcinomas are usually CK7−/CK20+/MUC2+/CDX2+, despite some immunophenotypic overlap [10]. For differentiating GCA from appendiceal neuroendocrine neoplasms, GCA shows positive staining for mucin with periodic acid-Schiff, aiding in its distinction from appendiceal neuroendocrine neoplasm. Additionally, the two tumors can be further distinguished by their proliferative indices, with GCA exhibiting a significantly higher Ki-67 index [11].Given its aggressive nature and potential for metastasis, treatment for appendix GCA typically involves a combination of surgery and chemotherapy. However, due to the rarity of GCA, there are no randomized control trials, and evidence-based guidelines for chemotherapy regimens are lacking. The available data often reference regimens used for colorectal cancer, such as FOLFOX (5-fluorouracil, leucovorin, and oxaliplatin) or XELOX. In advanced cases, cytoreductive surgery and heated intraperitoneal chemotherapy may be necessary. A Mayo Clinic review (1984–2004) followed 57 patients with GCA, 27 of whom received chemotherapy. Those treated with 5-FU-based regimens showed a trend toward longer survival, though the results were not statistically significant. For stage II and III patients, mean survival was 47 months with chemotherapy compared to 32 months without (p = 0.383). For stage IV patients, mean survival was 39 months with chemotherapy versus 29 months without (p = 0.281). Additional retrospective reviews and case series report various chemotherapy regimens and outcomes, summarized in Table 1. Based on the literature, adjuvant chemotherapy was recommended for our patient.Genetic studies in GCA have identified several frequent mutations, including ARID1A, ARID2, CDH1, RHPN2, and MLL2, while TP53 mutations were limited to poorly differentiated forms. Conventional colorectal adenocarcinoma mutations such as APC and KRAS were found less frequently [14]. Microsatellite instability, programmed death ligand 1, and tumor mutational burden are significant markers in cancer immunology. However, these markers are found at low rates in GCA, which accounts for its limited responsiveness to immunotherapies [15].In our case, there was no evidence of appendiceal metastases or abnormalities preoperatively. The patient underwent primary debulking surgery with a suspicious diagnosis of ovarian neoplasm. The frozen section returned as mucinous adenocarcinoma of the ovary, but distinguishing primary from metastatic lesions proved difficult. Then, we conducted a meticulous intraoperative exploration of the appendix and observed that it felt firm and thickened, despite its normal appearance. Appendectomy was performed, which subsequently led to the diagnosis of GCA of the appendix.

Conclusion

Appendiceal pathologies like GCA and other neoplasms can present as OC, even with a normal-appearing appendix, complicating diagnosis. This case report highlights the need to reconsider the role of appendectomy in MOC. Although routine appendectomy has a limited role in early OC staging, it should be considered during intraoperative exploration in MOC cases to distinguish appendiceal malignant metastasis.

Acknowledgments

We extend our gratitude to Dr. Zhe Chen for his invaluable editorial assistance in the preparation of this manuscript.

Statement of Ethics

This article does not contain any studies involving human participants or animals performed by any of the authors. Ethical approval was not required for this case study in accordance with local or national guidelines. Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for the publication of this case report and any accompanying images.

Conflict of Interest Statement

The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

Funding Sources

This study was not supported by any sponsor or funder.

Author Contributions

H.F. and R.G. designed the study, collected data, analyzed data, and wrote the manuscript. K.T., Y.T., and A.H. collected the data, analyzed data, and edited the manuscript. H.T., S.F., N.O., and O.Y. were involved in the critical revision of the manuscript for important intellectual content.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 55-year-old female (para 2).
- Chief Complaint: Elevated serum carcinoembryonic antigen levels and left ovarian tumor detected during routine health examination.
- History of Present Illness: None.
- Past Medical History: None.
- Personal History: None.
- Family History: None.
- Physical Examination: Intraoperatively, a 9-cm multinodular gray-white tumor was identified in the left ovary. The appendix felt firm and thickened despite appearing normal.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - Lab Results: Elevated serum carcinoembryonic antigen (34 ng/mL), mildly elevated alpha-fetoprotein (10.2 ng/mL), normal carbohydrate antigen 125 and 19-9 levels.
  - Imaging: 
    - MRI: Solid left ovarian tumor (62 × 44 mm).
    - PET/CT: Max standardized uptake value of 4.18 in left ovarian tumor; normal-appearing appendix.
  - Endoscopic Studies: Normal gastroscopy and colonoscopy.
  - Frozen Section: Mucinous adenocarcinoma of the ovary. Subsequent analysis identified goblet cell adenocarcinoma of the appendix with ovarian metastasis.
  - Immunohistochemical Results: Positive for CK7, CK20, CDX2; negative for chromogranin A and synaptophysin.
- Surgical Findings: 
  - Abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy.
  - Subsequent appendectomy confirmed appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma (staged as pT4aN1bM1a).
- Treatment: Ileocecal resection with D3 lymph node dissection and seven cycles of XELOX chemotherapy.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- Initial suspicion based on elevated tumor markers and left ovarian tumor included primary ovarian carcinoma and other gynecologic malignancies.
- Imaging, laboratory, and intraoperative findings raised the possibility of mucinous ovarian adenocarcinoma but could not conclusively rule out metastatic disease.
- Primary mucinous ovarian carcinoma was considered but was less likely after frozen section analysis and immunohistochemical results showed features consistent with gastrointestinal origin.
- Subsequent meticulous intraoperative exploration of the appendix revealed changes prompting appendectomy, confirming appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma with ovarian metastasis.
- Differential diagnoses such as other forms of appendiceal or neuroendocrine tumors were excluded based on histology (mucinous cells with nests and solid patterns) and immunohistochemistry.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma (GCA) with ovarian metastasis (staged as pT4aN1bM1a, AJCC/UICC TNM 8th).
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma (GCA) with ovarian metastasis.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Initial Assessment and Problem Identification: The patient is a 55-year-old female presenting with elevated CEA levels and a left ovarian tumor found during routine screening. This immediately raises suspicion for malignancy, particularly adenocarcinoma.

<step 2> Analyzing Ancillary Tests and Intraoperative Findings:  Further investigations, including MRI, PET/CT, and endoscopic studies, confirm the presence of a left ovarian tumor with metabolic activity. While gastroscopy and colonoscopy are normal, the intraoperative finding of a firm and thickened appendix is a significant clue that deviates from a primary ovarian tumor diagnosis.

<step 3> Interpreting Frozen Section and Immunohistochemistry: The initial frozen section diagnosis of mucinous adenocarcinoma of the ovary is a preliminary assessment based on the ovarian mass itself. However, subsequent immunohistochemical results (CK7+, CK20+, CDX2+, Chromogranin A-, Synaptophysin-) are more suggestive of an intestinal origin adenocarcinoma, rather than a typical primary ovarian mucinous tumor. This profile points towards a colorectal or appendiceal origin.

<step 4> Definitive Diagnosis from Appendectomy and Surgical Pathology: The crucial turning point is the appendectomy and its pathological analysis. The confirmation of goblet cell adenocarcinoma of the appendix with ovarian metastasis provides the definitive diagnosis.  Goblet cell adenocarcinomas of the appendix are known to metastasize to the ovaries, sometimes mimicking primary ovarian tumors (Krukenberg tumor). The staging (pT4aN1bM1a) confirms advanced disease with local invasion, regional lymph node metastasis, and distant metastasis to the ovary.

<step 5> Integrating All Findings and Final Diagnosis:  All the pieces of the puzzle fit together: elevated CEA, ovarian mass, immunohistochemical profile, and the definitive pathology of the appendix. The initial suspicion of a primary ovarian tumor was redirected by the intraoperative findings and refined by the subsequent pathological examinations to reveal the true primary tumor site as the appendix. The final diagnosis must reflect the primary site and the metastatic spread.

### Answer:
Goblet cell adenocarcinoma of the appendix with metastasis to the ovary.

reasoning_eval.step: Initial Assessment and Problem Identification: The patient is a 55-year-old female presenting with elevated CEA levels and a left ovarian tumor found during routine screening. This immediately raises suspicion for malignancy, particularly adenocarcinoma.

Analyzing Ancillary Tests and Intraoperative Findings:  Further investigations, including MRI, PET/CT, and endoscopic studies, confirm the presence of a left ovarian tumor with metabolic activity. While gastroscopy and colonoscopy are normal, the intraoperative finding of a firm and thickened appendix is a significant clue that deviates from a primary ovarian tumor diagnosis.

Interpreting Frozen Section and Immunohistochemistry: The initial frozen section diagnosis of mucinous adenocarcinoma of the ovary is a preliminary assessment based on the ovarian mass itself. However, subsequent immunohistochemical results (CK7+, CK20+, CDX2+, Chromogranin A-, Synaptophysin-) are more suggestive of an intestinal origin adenocarcinoma, rather than a typical primary ovarian mucinous tumor. This profile points towards a colorectal or appendiceal origin.

Definitive Diagnosis from Appendectomy and Surgical Pathology: The crucial turning point is the appendectomy and its pathological analysis. The confirmation of goblet cell adenocarcinoma of the appendix with ovarian metastasis provides the definitive diagnosis.  Goblet cell adenocarcinomas of the appendix are known to metastasize to the ovaries, sometimes mimicking primary ovarian tumors (Krukenberg tumor). The staging (pT4aN1bM1a) confirms advanced disease with local invasion, regional lymph node metastasis, and distant metastasis to the ovary.

Integrating All Findings and Final Diagnosis:  All the pieces of the puzzle fit together: elevated CEA, ovarian mass, immunohistochemical profile, and the definitive pathology of the appendix. The initial suspicion of a primary ovarian tumor was redirected by the intraoperative findings and refined by the subsequent pathological examinations to reveal the true primary tumor site as the appendix. The final diagnosis must reflect the primary site and the metastatic spread.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial suspicion based on elevated tumor markers and left ovarian tumor included primary ovarian carcinoma and other gynecologic malignancies.
Imaging, laboratory, and intraoperative findings raised the possibility of mucinous ovarian adenocarcinoma but could not conclusively rule out metastatic disease.
Primary mucinous ovarian carcinoma was considered but was less likely after frozen section analysis and immunohistochemical results showed features consistent with gastrointestinal origin.
Subsequent meticulous intraoperative exploration of the appendix revealed changes prompting appendectomy, confirming appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma with ovarian metastasis.
Differential diagnoses such as other forms of appendiceal or neuroendocrine tumors were excluded based on histology (mucinous cells with nests and solid patterns) and immunohistochemistry.
Final diagnosis: Appendiceal goblet cell adenocarcinoma (GCA) with ovarian metastasis (staged as pT4aN1bM1a, AJCC/UICC TNM 8th).
